narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted an 88.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application for our law school was unfortunately rejected—it's designed to prioritize students likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower rankings in your school's grading deciles from Year 1 and especially Year 3, where you placed in the bottom decile compared to your peers, pushing the model strongly toward expecting bar failure. Your LSAT score of 25 also played a major role, as it fell well below the typical score of around 37 seen in the dataset, further tipping the scales against passing. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA stood out positively above the average of 3.21, which helped pull the prediction somewhat toward success, showing your college-level academic strength. Your middle family income bracket had a small nudge toward failure risk compared to higher averages, while your full-time enrollment plans offered a minor positive influence, aligning with the vast majority of students who pursue that path. Overall, compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your profile showed weaker early-law school performance indicators that outweighed your strengths, unlike many who enter higher deciles and score better on the LSAT. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar situations have turned things around with targeted effort. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, seeking academic advising to improve your decile rankings if possible through additional coursework, and perhaps exploring part-time options or prep programs proven to lift bar passage rates. You're clearly capable, given your strong undergrad record, so focusing on these key areas could open doors at other schools or in future cycles. Keep pushing—you've got this.
